student_message: Oh cool! A plus sign has both superpowers because I can fold it perfectly and spin it around. Can you explain if 3D objects like a basketball have these same symmetry rules too?
final_message:
You are absolutely right about the plus sign having both superpowers! You are asking such wonderful questions, Zara. 

Yes, 3D objects have symmetry superpowers too! Instead of folding flat paper along a line, imagine slicing a perfectly plain basketball right down the middle. Both sides look exactly like identical little bowls, which creates a perfect 3D mirror. And just like your spinning flat shapes, think about a basketball player spinning the ball on their finger. Because it is perfectly round, it looks exactly the same the whole time it spins! 

So, a basketball has both mirror and spinning superpowers, just in a big, bouncy 3D way. Can you think of another 3D toy in your room that might have these same superpowers? 🏀

orchestrator_intent.goal: Explain how symmetry applies to 3D objects like a basketball.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The student is confident, focused, and asked a direct conceptual question about 3D symmetry. The dialogue agent is best suited to answer this.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
session_summary: Zara covered rotational ("spinning") and reflectional ("folding") symmetry. She understood the concepts exceptionally well, correctly identifying that letters like 'Z' have only rotational symmetry, while squares and plus signs possess both. Zara exhibited no struggles during this session. Her current learning state is highly engaged and progressive; having firmly grasped 2D symmetry rules, she is now proactively inquiring about how symmetry applies to 3D objects like basketballs.
